env.ae.low_spatial_frequency_content.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Low Spatial Frequency Content

semantics.definition: Use when visual information is concentrated in broad shapes and uniform surfaces, with minimal fine textures. This is about scale of detail and corresponds to low spatial frequency energy. Apply to minimalist or smooth environments where details are large and simple. Do not use for scenes with intricate textures or many small objects. This tag contrasts with high_spatial_frequency_content.
semantics.definition_long: Use when visual information is concentrated in broad shapes and uniform surfaces, with minimal fine textures. This is about scale of detail and corresponds to low spatial frequency energy. Apply to minimalist or smooth environments where details are large and simple. Do not use for scenes with intricate textures or many small objects. This tag contrasts with high_spatial_frequency_content.
semantics.definition_short: The scene is dominated by large, smooth forms with little fine-scale detail.
examples_negative: A room with fine patterned wallpaper and detailed trim.
A cluttered shop with many small items.
No clear evidence of Low Spatial Frequency Content is present.
examples_positive: A minimal lobby with large plain walls and floor surfaces.
A space with big blocks of color and few details.
Clear evidence of Low Spatial Frequency Content is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use spatial frequency analysis to confirm energy is concentrated in low-frequency bands. Large uniform regions with few small edges indicate low frequency content. Ensure that low frequency is not due to blur; check for focus and resolution. Apply when simplicity is structural, not accidental.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze texture scale and normal variation across surfaces. Low spatial frequency content appears as smooth gradients and large flat surfaces. Tag when fine detail is scarce across the main view.
scope_excludes: fine-grained patterns
dense small objects
intricate ornamentation
scope_includes: large uniform planes
simple geometric forms
broad color fields
minimal detail interiors

semantics.definition: Use when the composition or layout does not show noticeable bilateral or radial symmetry. The arrangement of elements is uneven, asymmetrical, or varied without mirroring. Apply when asymmetry is a dominant visual characteristic. Do not use when there is clear symmetry even if minor irregularities exist. This tag is the counterpart of high_symmetry.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the composition or layout does not show noticeable bilateral or radial symmetry. The arrangement of elements is uneven, asymmetrical, or varied without mirroring. Apply when asymmetry is a dominant visual characteristic. Do not use when there is clear symmetry even if minor irregularities exist. This tag is the counterpart of high_symmetry.
semantics.definition_short: The scene lacks clear symmetry and appears visually unbalanced or irregularly arranged.
examples_negative: A hallway with identical elements on both sides.
A rotunda with evenly spaced columns.
No clear evidence of Low Symmetry is present.
examples_positive: A room with furniture placed unevenly and no mirrored sides.
A corridor with mismatched openings and fixtures.
Clear evidence of Low Symmetry is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Compute symmetry scores and confirm low alignment between mirrored halves. Visual imbalance and irregular spacing support this tag. Ensure that the lack of symmetry is global, not just a small region. Avoid tagging when symmetry is apparent despite minor variations.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Evaluate spatial symmetry in object positions and plan geometry. Low symmetry scores across primary axes indicate applicability. Tag when asymmetry is pervasive in layout.
scope_excludes: clear mirror symmetry
radial layouts
balanced compositions
scope_includes: asymmetric furnishings
irregular spatial layouts
uneven distribution of elements
varied forms without mirroring

env.ae.low_texture_complexity.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Low Texture Complexity

semantics.definition: Use when most visible materials are flat, polished, or uniformly finished with little surface detail. The space appears visually smooth and not materially rich. Apply when multiple dominant surfaces lack texture. Do not use for spaces with rough, layered, or patterned materials. This tag is the counterpart of high_texture_complexity.
semantics.definition_long: Use when most visible materials are flat, polished, or uniformly finished with little surface detail. The space appears visually smooth and not materially rich. Apply when multiple dominant surfaces lack texture. Do not use for spaces with rough, layered, or patterned materials. This tag is the counterpart of high_texture_complexity.
semantics.definition_short: Surfaces are largely smooth and uniform with minimal texture variation.
examples_negative: A space with exposed brick and rough stone.
A room with textured wallpaper and patterned floors.
No clear evidence of Low Texture Complexity is present.
examples_positive: A room with smooth painted walls and polished concrete floors.
A minimalist gallery with plain white surfaces.
Clear evidence of Low Texture Complexity is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for low micro-contrast on surfaces and minimal texture cues. Uniform reflectance and lack of surface detail indicate low texture complexity. Ensure that texture absence is not due to blur or low resolution. Apply when smoothness is pervasive.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material roughness and normal maps to estimate surface variation. Low texture complexity shows low variance in normals and albedo. Tag when dominant surfaces are smooth and uniform.
scope_excludes: rough stone or brick
textured plaster
patterned or layered materials
scope_includes: smooth painted surfaces
polished or matte uniform materials
plain drywall
featureless floors

semantics.definition: Use when circulation paths are hard to predict due to frequent turns, partitions, or branching corridors. The space feels maze-like rather than open or legible. Apply when multiple possible routes compete and sightlines are broken. Do not use for clearly linear corridors or open plans. This tag indicates navigational complexity.
semantics.definition_long: Use when circulation paths are hard to predict due to frequent turns, partitions, or branching corridors. The space feels maze-like rather than open or legible. Apply when multiple possible routes compete and sightlines are broken. Do not use for clearly linear corridors or open plans. This tag indicates navigational complexity.
semantics.definition_short: The layout is complex and confusing, with many turns or branches that obscure the overall path.
examples_negative: A straight corridor with clear endpoints.
An open lobby with obvious routes.
No clear evidence of Maze-Like Layout is present.
examples_positive: A building interior with many short corridors and turns.
A retail space with winding aisles and limited visibility.
Clear evidence of Maze-Like Layout is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for repeated turns, occlusions, and branching paths in the image. Visual cues include segmented corridors and walls that block long views. Avoid tagging based on clutter alone; focus on path structure. Use architectural lines to infer route complexity.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze the navigation graph for high branching factor and low visibility between nodes. Compute path complexity metrics such as average turns per shortest path. Tag when circulation requires frequent turns or decisions.
scope_excludes: linear hallways
open-plan circulation
clear and direct routes
scope_includes: multiple corridor branches
winding or zigzag paths
short sightlines with frequent turns
partitioned circulation

semantics.definition: Use when metal elements occupy a substantial portion of the view, such as metal panels, railings, or structural elements. The material should read clearly as metal through reflectance or texture cues. Apply when metal is a primary finish or structural feature, not just small accents. Do not use for minor metal hardware. This tag is about material prominence.
semantics.definition_long: Use when metal elements occupy a substantial portion of the view, such as metal panels, railings, or structural elements. The material should read clearly as metal through reflectance or texture cues. Apply when metal is a primary finish or structural feature, not just small accents. Do not use for minor metal hardware. This tag is about material prominence.
semantics.definition_short: Metal surfaces are a dominant visible material in the scene.
examples_negative: A room with only small metal door handles.
A space where metal is barely visible.
No clear evidence of Metal Prominent is present.
examples_positive: An interior with large brushed metal wall panels.
A space with exposed steel beams and metal railings.
Clear evidence of Metal Prominent is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for metallic reflectance, specular highlights, and characteristic textures like brushed or polished metal. Confirm that metal covers significant area or is visually dominant. Avoid confusing grey paint with metal. Use contextual cues like exposed steel members.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material classifications to identify metal surfaces and compute their visible area. Tag when metal surfaces exceed a threshold of the visible field. Combine with reflectance properties to confirm metallic materials.
scope_excludes: small hardware
painted surfaces that are not metal
minor metal accents
scope_includes: metal wall panels
steel beams
metal railings
metal ceilings

semantics.definition: Use when there are few or no visible signs, labels, or wayfinding elements in the environment. The space appears largely unmarked, with minimal text or directional cues. Apply when the absence of signage is notable relative to the space type. Do not use if signs are present but small or unreadable due to distance; use normal visibility judgment. This tag is about quantity of signage, not branding.
semantics.definition_long: Use when there are few or no visible signs, labels, or wayfinding elements in the environment. The space appears largely unmarked, with minimal text or directional cues. Apply when the absence of signage is notable relative to the space type. Do not use if signs are present but small or unreadable due to distance; use normal visibility judgment. This tag is about quantity of signage, not branding.
semantics.definition_short: Signage and labeled wayfinding elements are scarce or absent in the scene.
examples_negative: An airport concourse with many directional signs.
A hospital hallway with labeled rooms and exit signage.
No clear evidence of Minimal Signage is present.
examples_positive: A corridor with blank walls and no door labels.
A lobby with no visible directory or exit signs.
Clear evidence of Minimal Signage is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use text detection and sign detectors to count signage elements. Minimal signage shows very low sign count relative to scene size. Confirm that signs are not present but simply out of frame by checking visible walls and doors. Avoid false negatives when lighting or resolution hides small signs.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories for signs and labels to count signage density per area. Tag when signage count is below a threshold for the space type. Consider visibility and distance for sign readability.
scope_excludes: prominent wayfinding signage
room labels or directories
brand signage visible
scope_includes: absence of exit signs
few or no labels
blank walls without signage
unmarked doors

semantics.definition: Use when the space is characterized by a single dominant color or a very limited set of hues. The palette should feel restrained and minimal rather than varied. Apply to monochrome or near-monochrome interiors with little color variation. Do not use when multiple distinct hues are present. This tag is about palette simplicity.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space is characterized by a single dominant color or a very limited set of hues. The palette should feel restrained and minimal rather than varied. Apply to monochrome or near-monochrome interiors with little color variation. Do not use when multiple distinct hues are present. This tag is about palette simplicity.
semantics.definition_short: The palette is minimal or monochrome, with very few hues dominating the scene.
examples_negative: A room with multiple bold colors.
A space with varied decor hues.
No clear evidence of Monochrome / Minimal Palette is present.
examples_positive: A white-on-white gallery space with minimal color variation.
A room dominated by grey tones with no strong accents.
Clear evidence of Monochrome / Minimal Palette is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue histogram and verify a narrow distribution with few peaks. Monochrome palettes show low hue variance even if brightness varies. Confirm that accents are minimal and not dominant. Avoid tagging when small but vivid accents significantly change the palette.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors and compute hue variance across major surfaces. Tag when hue variance is low and one hue dominates. Consider lighting that shifts perceived hue across surfaces.
scope_excludes: multi-colored palettes
high color entropy
varied hue distributions
scope_includes: single dominant hue
very limited colors
tonal variations of one color
minimal accent color

semantics.definition: Use when the corridor width is tight relative to human scale, restricting movement or creating a constricted feel. The key cue is lateral clearance, not ceiling height. Apply when walls are close together along the path. Do not use for standard-width or wide hallways. This tag focuses on corridor geometry, not crowding.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the corridor width is tight relative to human scale, restricting movement or creating a constricted feel. The key cue is lateral clearance, not ceiling height. Apply when walls are close together along the path. Do not use for standard-width or wide hallways. This tag focuses on corridor geometry, not crowding.
semantics.definition_short: The corridor is notably narrow, with limited width for passage.
examples_negative: A wide lobby corridor with ample space.
An open walkway with broad clearance.
No clear evidence of Narrow Corridor is present.
examples_positive: A hallway where two people could barely pass.
A service corridor with close walls and minimal clearance.
Clear evidence of Narrow Corridor is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Estimate corridor width using door frames, people, or furniture as scale references. Narrow corridors show strong perspective with close parallel walls. Check lateral clearance relative to typical human shoulder width. Avoid tagging when perspective exaggeration makes width unclear.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Measure corridor width from geometry and compare to human scale thresholds. Tag when width falls below a defined narrow range. Consider obstructions that further reduce effective width.
scope_excludes: standard corridors
open galleries
wide circulation zones
scope_includes: service corridors
tight passageways
narrow hallways in older buildings
constricted connecting paths

semantics.definition: Use when the scene is dominated by earth tones like brown, tan, olive, terracotta, or muted green. The palette should feel natural and organic rather than saturated or synthetic. Apply when these hues cover major surfaces or decor elements. Do not use for vivid or high-contrast palettes. This tag is about overall color impression.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene is dominated by earth tones like brown, tan, olive, terracotta, or muted green. The palette should feel natural and organic rather than saturated or synthetic. Apply when these hues cover major surfaces or decor elements. Do not use for vivid or high-contrast palettes. This tag is about overall color impression.
semantics.definition_short: The overall palette emphasizes natural, earthy hues such as browns, greens, and muted warm tones.
examples_negative: A room dominated by bright primary colors.
A monochrome black and white space.
No clear evidence of Natural / Earthy Palette is present.
examples_positive: A space with tan walls, wood finishes, and olive textiles.
A cafe with terracotta tiles and muted green accents.
Clear evidence of Natural / Earthy Palette is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Compute hue distribution and check for dominance of browns, tans, muted greens, and warm earth tones. Ensure these hues cover large areas, not just small accents. Avoid tagging if bright or cool colors dominate. Use material cues like wood or stone to support the palette.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Aggregate material colors across major surfaces and compare to an earth-tone palette range. Tag when earth-tone coverage exceeds a threshold. Consider lighting that may shift hue perception.
scope_excludes: highly saturated palettes
neon or synthetic colors
cool monochrome schemes
scope_includes: wood and tan finishes
muted greens
terracotta or clay tones
natural stone colors

semantics.definition: Use when architectural or design elements feature organic, flowing, or biomorphic shapes. The forms should evoke natural patterns like curves, branching, or irregular organic contours. Apply when these shapes are prominent in the space. Do not use for purely geometric curves or standard rectilinear forms. This tag is about nature-inspired form language.
semantics.definition_long: Use when architectural or design elements feature organic, flowing, or biomorphic shapes. The forms should evoke natural patterns like curves, branching, or irregular organic contours. Apply when these shapes are prominent in the space. Do not use for purely geometric curves or standard rectilinear forms. This tag is about nature-inspired form language.
semantics.definition_short: Forms mimic organic, nature-inspired shapes rather than strict geometry.
examples_negative: A room with only straight, rectilinear walls.
A space with simple circular columns but no organic cues.
No clear evidence of Naturalistic Forms is present.
examples_positive: A ceiling with branching, tree-like structural forms.
Furniture with smooth, organic contours inspired by nature.
Clear evidence of Naturalistic Forms is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for flowing contours and irregular organic shapes that resemble natural forms. Use shape descriptors to detect non-orthogonal, non-circular organic outlines. Confirm that naturalistic shapes are dominant, not minor accents. Avoid tagging on generic curves without organic cues.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface curvature and form descriptors for organic, biomorphic shapes. Tag when a significant portion of geometry deviates from simple primitives and resembles natural forms. Use clustering of curvature patterns to validate.
scope_excludes: purely rectilinear geometry
simple geometric curves
faceted or angular forms
scope_includes: biomorphic structures
branching forms
organic curves
nature-inspired shapes

semantics.definition: Use when the space includes artworks, prints, murals, or graphics depicting natural elements like forests, mountains, water, or plants. The nature imagery should be clearly recognizable and intentional. Apply to posters, murals, digital displays, or printed panels. Do not use for real plants or natural materials; use indoor_plants or natural materials tags instead. This tag is about depicted nature.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space includes artworks, prints, murals, or graphics depicting natural elements like forests, mountains, water, or plants. The nature imagery should be clearly recognizable and intentional. Apply to posters, murals, digital displays, or printed panels. Do not use for real plants or natural materials; use indoor_plants or natural materials tags instead. This tag is about depicted nature.
semantics.definition_short: Images or representations of nature are visible, such as landscapes, plants, or natural scenes.
examples_negative: Real potted plants in a room.
Abstract art with no clear natural subject.
No clear evidence of Nature Imagery is present.
examples_positive: A wall mural showing a forest landscape.
Framed photos of mountains in a corridor.
Clear evidence of Nature Imagery is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use image classification or OCR context to identify depicted natural scenes. Look for recognizable natural elements in artwork frames or panels. Distinguish between real plants and images of plants. Ensure the imagery is visible and intentional.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Identify art assets or texture maps that depict nature. Use object metadata for wall art or displays to detect nature themes. Tag when nature imagery is visible from main viewpoints.
scope_excludes: real vegetation
abstract patterns without nature
material textures like wood grain
scope_includes: landscape murals
nature-themed posters
photographs of natural scenes
digital displays with nature imagery

semantics.definition: Use when visual access is repeatedly blocked by walls, partitions, or tall objects, resulting in short or interrupted sightlines. The key is reduced visual permeability across the space. Apply when occlusions dominate the view and prevent seeing far. Do not use when sightlines are clear even if circulation is complex. This tag is the counterpart of clear_sightlines.
semantics.definition_long: Use when visual access is repeatedly blocked by walls, partitions, or tall objects, resulting in short or interrupted sightlines. The key is reduced visual permeability across the space. Apply when occlusions dominate the view and prevent seeing far. Do not use when sightlines are clear even if circulation is complex. This tag is the counterpart of clear_sightlines.
semantics.definition_short: Views through the space are blocked by partitions or objects, limiting long sightlines.
examples_negative: A long open corridor with a visible endpoint.
An open-plan lobby with clear views across the space.
No clear evidence of Occluded Sightlines is present.
examples_positive: An office with tall partitions that block views across the floor.
A maze-like corridor where turns and walls block the endpoint.
Clear evidence of Occluded Sightlines is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Estimate visibility distance using depth cues and occlusion rate. Frequent vertical occlusions at eye level indicate blocked sightlines. Look for interrupted vanishing lines and short view corridors. Avoid tagging when obstructions are low or transparent.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Compute isovist area or visibility graphs from occupant positions. Occluded sightlines show low isovist area and short visibility distances. Tag when visual permeability metrics are below threshold across typical viewpoints.
scope_excludes: open-plan layouts
glass partitions that preserve visibility
long unobstructed corridors
scope_includes: tall partitions
frequent wall breaks
screened areas blocking views
dense vertical obstructions

semantics.definition: Use when the space is largely continuous, with minimal full-height walls separating functions. The area reads as one shared volume, even if zones are defined by furniture. Apply to open offices, lofts, or large rooms without enclosed rooms. Do not use for compartmentalized or cell-based layouts. This tag is about plan openness.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space is largely continuous, with minimal full-height walls separating functions. The area reads as one shared volume, even if zones are defined by furniture. Apply to open offices, lofts, or large rooms without enclosed rooms. Do not use for compartmentalized or cell-based layouts. This tag is about plan openness.
semantics.definition_short: The layout is open with few full-height partitions, creating a continuous shared space.
examples_negative: A clinic with many small rooms off a corridor.
A house with separate enclosed rooms.
No clear evidence of Open Plan is present.
examples_positive: An office floor with desks in a single open area.
A loft with kitchen, dining, and living zones in one room.
Clear evidence of Open Plan is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for long sightlines and absence of full-height walls separating areas. Furniture groupings may define zones but should not block visibility. Use wall detection to estimate partition density. Avoid tagging if rooms are clearly separated by walls and doors.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze the navigable space as a single large cell with few partitions. Tag when the number of enclosed rooms is low relative to area. Use wall height data to differentiate full-height partitions from low dividers.
scope_excludes: many enclosed rooms
full-height partitions
cellular layouts
scope_includes: open office floors
loft-style interiors
large shared halls
spaces divided mainly by furniture

semantics.definition: Use when window panels are visibly open, such as casement windows swung out or sliding windows retracted. The opening should be clear and not merely implied. Apply to scenes where open windows are visible and intentional. Do not use for closed windows or fixed glazing. This tag is about physical openness.
semantics.definition_long: Use when window panels are visibly open, such as casement windows swung out or sliding windows retracted. The opening should be clear and not merely implied. Apply to scenes where open windows are visible and intentional. Do not use for closed windows or fixed glazing. This tag is about physical openness.
semantics.definition_short: Windows are open, allowing direct airflow or connection to the exterior.
examples_negative: Closed windows with no visible opening.
A glass curtain wall without operable windows.
No clear evidence of Open Windows is present.
examples_positive: A room with casement windows swung outward.
A corridor with sliding windows open to the outside.
Clear evidence of Open Windows is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for window frames and panels that are visibly displaced from the closed position. Gaps with visible exterior air or angle of window sashes indicate openness. Confirm that the opening is a window, not a door. Avoid tagging when only blinds are open.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use window object states or hinge angles to detect openness. Tag when operable windows have non-zero open angles or retracted panels. Consider airflow indicators like curtains moving only as secondary cues.
scope_excludes: closed glazing
fixed windows
doors used instead of windows
scope_includes: open casement windows
open sliding windows
tilted open windows
open clerestory windows

env.ae.poor_visibility_/_dark_corners.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Poor Visibility / Dark Corners

semantics.definition: Use when portions of the space are visibly underlit, with dark corners or areas where details are hard to discern. The cue is uneven or inadequate lighting that reduces visibility. Apply when dark zones are notable rather than a minor shadow. Do not use for stylistic low lighting that still preserves visibility, unless dark areas obscure details. This tag can co-occur with low_illuminance but emphasizes localized dark zones.
semantics.definition_long: Use when portions of the space are visibly underlit, with dark corners or areas where details are hard to discern. The cue is uneven or inadequate lighting that reduces visibility. Apply when dark zones are notable rather than a minor shadow. Do not use for stylistic low lighting that still preserves visibility, unless dark areas obscure details. This tag can co-occur with low_illuminance but emphasizes localized dark zones.
semantics.definition_short: Lighting is insufficient in parts of the scene, creating dark corners or areas with low visibility.
examples_negative: Evenly lit space with no dark areas.
A scene with strong lighting but no obscured corners.
No clear evidence of Poor Visibility / Dark Corners is present.
examples_positive: A corridor with unlit corners where details are hard to see.
A room with bright center lighting but dark perimeter areas.
Clear evidence of Poor Visibility / Dark Corners is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect low-luminance regions adjacent to brighter areas and measure local contrast. Dark corners often occur near walls or alcoves with minimal light spill. Confirm that detail loss is present, not just darker color. Avoid tagging when shadows are mild and detail remains clear.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use lighting simulation or luminance sampling to identify zones below visibility thresholds. Compute variance in illuminance and flag regions with low lux values. Tag when a significant portion of the view is underlit relative to the main area.
scope_excludes: uniform low light without distinct dark corners
bright, evenly lit spaces
stylized darkness with clear visibility
scope_includes: dark corner areas
uneven lighting creating visibility gaps
underlit alcoves
shadowed perimeters

semantics.definition: Use when there is a clearly defined niche, alcove, or recess that provides a sense of privacy or enclosure. The niche should feel separated from primary circulation or open exposure. It can be created by walls, screens, furniture wings, or architectural recesses. Apply even if no person is present. Do not use for open seating without enclosing boundaries.
semantics.definition_long: Use when there is a clearly defined niche, alcove, or recess that provides a sense of privacy or enclosure. The niche should feel separated from primary circulation or open exposure. It can be created by walls, screens, furniture wings, or architectural recesses. Apply even if no person is present. Do not use for open seating without enclosing boundaries.
semantics.definition_short: A small, partially enclosed spot offers privacy or seclusion within a larger space.
examples_negative: Open bench seating along a main corridor.
A large open lounge with no enclosure.
No clear evidence of Private / Enclosed Niche is present.
examples_positive: A window alcove with side walls and a built-in bench.
A booth recessed into a wall with high side panels.
Clear evidence of Private / Enclosed Niche is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for small regions bounded on at least two sides with a visible opening to the main space. Enclosure cues include side walls, screens, or high-backed seating. The niche should appear secondary to the main area and not a full room. Avoid tagging when the area is fully exposed.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use proximity to surrounding surfaces to compute local enclosure or refuge scores. Tag when a sub-area has high enclosure and low exposure relative to the main space. Use ray casting to confirm limited sightlines into the niche from main circulation.
scope_excludes: open-plan seating
transparent-only separation
full rooms with doors
scope_includes: alcoves and bays
recessed seating
screened nooks
small enclosed corners

semantics.definition: Use when a space reads as open, exposed, and lacking privacy, such as a central plaza or wide open lobby. The key cue is low enclosure and high visibility from multiple directions. Apply to areas intended for public gathering or passage with minimal visual protection. Do not use for semi-enclosed or screened spaces. This tag contrasts with private/enclosed niches.
semantics.definition_long: Use when a space reads as open, exposed, and lacking privacy, such as a central plaza or wide open lobby. The key cue is low enclosure and high visibility from multiple directions. Apply to areas intended for public gathering or passage with minimal visual protection. Do not use for semi-enclosed or screened spaces. This tag contrasts with private/enclosed niches.
semantics.definition_short: An open area is highly exposed and lacks enclosure or shielding from views.
examples_negative: A seating area with screens and partial walls.
A recessed lounge tucked behind columns.
No clear evidence of Public / Exposed Area is present.
examples_positive: A central atrium floor with no partitions or screens.
A large open concourse visible from multiple levels.
Clear evidence of Public / Exposed Area is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for large contiguous open floor area with minimal boundary elements. High visibility across the space and lack of nearby shielding are strong cues. Use perspective to estimate how many directions the area is exposed to. Avoid confusing openness with emptiness if there is clear enclosure.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Compute isovist area or visibility graph for the zone. Tag when local enclosure is low and visibility from multiple directions is high. Use distance to nearest occluding surfaces to measure exposure.
scope_excludes: alcoves or niches
screened seating
spaces with strong enclosure
scope_includes: open plazas or lobbies
central circulation zones
large open concourses
open gathering areas

semantics.definition: Use when the space is defined by straight lines and right angles, with boxy or orthogonal geometry dominating. The overall impression should be rectilinear rather than curved or faceted. Apply to standard rooms, offices, or modern interiors with orthogonal planning. Do not use when curvilinear or complex geometry is prominent. This tag is about dominant form language.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space is defined by straight lines and right angles, with boxy or orthogonal geometry dominating. The overall impression should be rectilinear rather than curved or faceted. Apply to standard rooms, offices, or modern interiors with orthogonal planning. Do not use when curvilinear or complex geometry is prominent. This tag is about dominant form language.
semantics.definition_short: Architectural forms are predominantly rectilinear, with orthogonal walls, edges, and planar geometry.
examples_negative: A space with sweeping curved walls.
A hall with faceted, angled surfaces.
No clear evidence of Rectilinear Forms is present.
examples_positive: A room with straight walls, flat ceiling, and right-angle corners.
An office with rectangular partitions and orthogonal layout.
Clear evidence of Rectilinear Forms is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect dominant line orientations and confirm strong horizontal and vertical edges. Right-angle corners and rectangular openings are key cues. Ensure curvilinear elements are minimal or secondary. Avoid tagging when diagonal or curved features dominate the view.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use surface normals and edge directions to assess orthogonality. Tag when most surfaces align with orthogonal axes. Compute angular variance and confirm a rectilinear dominant geometry.
scope_excludes: curved surfaces
angled or faceted geometry
irregular forms
scope_includes: orthogonal walls
rectangular openings
straight-line geometry
boxy volumes

semantics.definition: Use when clearly identifiable religious symbols, objects, or iconography are present, such as crosses, crescents, statues, or altars. The symbols should be intentional and visible. Apply to worship spaces or rooms with religious decor. Do not use for ambiguous shapes without clear religious meaning. This tag is about visible religious iconography.
semantics.definition_long: Use when clearly identifiable religious symbols, objects, or iconography are present, such as crosses, crescents, statues, or altars. The symbols should be intentional and visible. Apply to worship spaces or rooms with religious decor. Do not use for ambiguous shapes without clear religious meaning. This tag is about visible religious iconography.
semantics.definition_short: Religious symbols or iconography are visible in the space.
examples_negative: Abstract art with no religious motifs.
A room with generic geometric decor.
No clear evidence of Religious Symbols is present.
examples_positive: A church interior with a prominent cross on the wall.
A prayer room with visible religious symbols and artifacts.
Clear evidence of Religious Symbols is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Use symbol detection and context cues to identify religious icons. Look for recognizable shapes and associated artifacts. Confirm that symbols are intentional and central enough to notice. Avoid tagging when shapes are ambiguous or purely decorative.

semantics.definition: Use when a visible pattern or motif repeats at a consistent scale, spacing, or rhythm across a surface or array of elements. The repetition should be perceptible and systematic. Apply to tiles, panels, screens, or repeated structural elements. Do not use for irregular or random patterning. This tag is about regular repetition, not complexity.
semantics.definition_long: Use when a visible pattern or motif repeats at a consistent scale, spacing, or rhythm across a surface or array of elements. This tag is about regular repetition, not complexity.

semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface tiling or repeated geometry modules. Compute periodicity and uniform spacing across surfaces. Tag when repetition is regular and covers a significant portion of the view.

random texture

semantics.definition: Use when chairs, benches, stools, or other seating objects are visible and appear intended for use. This tag is about availability, not density or comfort. semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object class labels to identify seating meshes. Tag when at least one seating object is visible in the primary field of view. Optionally check accessibility to ensure seats are not blocked.

decor items without seating function
hidden seating out of view
scope_includes: chairs and benches

scope_includes: sofas and armchairs

semantics.definition: Use when the space appears empty or nearly empty of people. The key cue is low person count relative to the space size. Apply to scenes with zero or very few people visible. Do not use when many people are present, even if the space is large. This tag is about current occupancy, not capacity.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space appears empty or nearly empty of people. The key cue is low person count relative to the space size. Apply to scenes with zero or very few people visible. Do not use when many people are present, even if the space is large. This tag is about current occupancy, not capacity.

semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect people and estimate count per area. Sparse occupancy shows zero or very low person density across the frame. Ensure people are not obscured by occlusions or framing. Avoid tagging when people are present but partially hidden in large numbers.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use agent counts or person detections in the scene graph. Tag when the number of visible occupants is below a threshold for the space size. Consider line of sight to avoid missing occupants behind obstacles.

moderate or high occupancy
people-dense scenes
scope_includes: empty halls

few visible occupants

The landmark should be visually salient by size, contrast, or uniqueness. Apply to sculptures, reception desks, atrium features, or central installations. Do not use for peripheral features or subtle decor. This tag emphasizes centrality and salience.

semantics.definition: Use when horizontal elements such as long bands, slabs, or layered lines are the primary visual cue. The emphasis is on horizontal directionality and alignment. Apply to spaces with strong horizontal datum lines, low-slung volumes, or repeated horizontal bands. Do not use when vertical or diagonal elements dominate. This tag is about directional emphasis, not symmetry.
semantics.definition_long: Use when horizontal elements such as long bands, slabs, or layered lines are the primary visual cue. The emphasis is on horizontal directionality and alignment. Apply to spaces with strong horizontal datum lines, low-slung volumes, or repeated horizontal bands. Do not use when vertical or diagonal elements dominate. semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Analyze surface edges and structural lines for orientation distribution. Tag when horizontal edges significantly exceed vertical or diagonal counts. Use geometry normals and edge direction statistics.

semantics.definition_long: Use when vertical elements such as columns, tall walls, or elongated features are the primary visual cue. semantics.definition: Use when illumination has a warm cast, typical of incandescent or warm LED lighting. Whites and neutrals should appear slightly yellow or amber. Apply when the lighting temperature is clearly warm across the scene. Do not use when lighting is cool or neutral. This tag concerns light color, not surface color.
semantics.definition_long: Use when illumination has a warm cast, typical of incandescent or warm LED lighting. Whites and neutrals should appear slightly yellow or amber. Apply when the lighting temperature is clearly warm across the scene. Do not use when lighting is cool or neutral. This tag concerns light color, not surface color.

Confirm that the cast is consistent across the frame. Avoid tagging when warmth comes from surface colors rather than light.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use light source color temperature metadata where available. Tag when dominant lights have low color temperature values. Validate by sampling rendered white surfaces for warm bias.
scope_excludes: cool lighting
neutral daylight
colored lighting unrelated to warmth
scope_includes: warm LED lighting
incandescent lighting
amber light fixtures
warm ambient light

semantics.definition: Use when surfaces, fixtures, and furnishings show little to no visible damage, stains, or deterioration. This is about condition and upkeep rather than cleanliness alone. Apply when the overall impression is cared-for and functional. Do not use if there are notable cracks, peeling paint, or broken fixtures. This tag can co-occur with high_cleanliness but is distinct.
semantics.definition_long: Use when surfaces, fixtures, and furnishings show little to no visible damage, stains, or deterioration. This is about condition and upkeep rather than cleanliness alone. Apply when the overall impression is cared-for and functional. Do not use if there are notable cracks, peeling paint, or broken fixtures. This tag can co-occur with high_cleanliness but is distinct.
semantics.definition_short: The space appears maintained and in good repair, with minimal visible wear or damage.
examples_negative: A room with peeling paint and broken tiles.
A space with damaged furniture and worn surfaces.
No clear evidence of Well Maintained is present.
examples_positive: A hallway with intact finishes and no visible damage.
A stairwell with clean, unbroken handrails and walls.
Clear evidence of Well Maintained is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for cues of wear such as chipped paint, cracks, stains, or missing components. A consistent, intact finish across surfaces indicates good maintenance. Distinguish from cleanliness by focusing on damage rather than dirt. Avoid tagging if only a small area is maintained but others are damaged.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material condition annotations or defect detection models to identify damage. Tag when defect density is low across visible surfaces. Consider both structural elements and furniture condition.
scope_excludes: cracked or peeling surfaces
broken fixtures
visible neglect
scope_includes: intact finishes
working fixtures
undamaged walls and floors
no visible deterioration

semantics.definition: Use when wood surfaces cover a substantial portion of the visible area and strongly influence the material impression. This includes wood floors, wall panels, ceilings, or large furniture pieces. Apply when wood reads as a primary finish rather than minor accents. Do not use for small wooden items only. The tag focuses on material prominence.
semantics.definition_long: Use when wood surfaces cover a substantial portion of the visible area and strongly influence the material impression. This includes wood floors, wall panels, ceilings, or large furniture pieces. Apply when wood reads as a primary finish rather than minor accents. Do not use for small wooden items only. The tag focuses on material prominence.
semantics.definition_short: Wood is a dominant visible material in the scene.
examples_negative: A room with only a small wooden table.
A concrete space with minor wood trim.
No clear evidence of Wood Prominent is present.
examples_positive: A space with wood floors and wood wall paneling.
A room dominated by timber beams and wood ceiling.
Clear evidence of Wood Prominent is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for wood grain textures and warm brown hues across large surfaces. Confirm that wood covers a significant area, not just small objects. Use material classification to distinguish wood from wood-look finishes when possible. Avoid tagging if wood is limited to trim.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material metadata to identify wood surfaces and compute visible area proportion. Tag when wood surfaces exceed a threshold of the view. Combine with texture cues such as grain patterns to validate.
scope_excludes: small wood accents
wood patterns on non-wood materials
minor wooden furniture
scope_includes: wood floors
wood wall panels
timber ceilings
exposed timber structure

env.ae.work_surfaces_present.definition: Article Eater antecedent: Work Surfaces Present

semantics.definition: Use when surfaces suitable for working, writing, or task activities are clearly visible. The surfaces should be functional and accessible, not just decorative consoles. Apply to offices, studios, labs, or work areas with desks or counters. Do not use when only small side tables or decorative shelves are present. This tag is about the presence of work-capable surfaces.
semantics.definition_long: Use when surfaces suitable for working, writing, or task activities are clearly visible. The surfaces should be functional and accessible, not just decorative consoles. Apply to offices, studios, labs, or work areas with desks or counters. Do not use when only small side tables or decorative shelves are present. This tag is about the presence of work-capable surfaces.
semantics.definition_short: Work surfaces such as desks, tables, or counters are visible and intended for tasks.
examples_negative: A lounge with only coffee tables.
A hallway with a narrow decorative console.
No clear evidence of Work Surfaces Present is present.
examples_positive: An office with desks and chairs arranged for work.
A workshop with long workbenches.
Clear evidence of Work Surfaces Present is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect desk or table objects and verify scale consistent with working surfaces. Work surfaces often have chairs, monitors, or task tools nearby. Confirm accessibility and placement for use. Avoid tagging from tiny tables used for decor.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use object categories and dimensions to identify work surfaces. Tag when at least one desk or workbench is visible and accessible. Use proximity to task objects to reinforce classification.
scope_excludes: small side tables
decorative consoles
surfaces not intended for work
scope_includes: desks and work tables
work benches
task counters
lab work surfaces

semantics.definition: Use when the space exhibits visible wear such as scuffs, peeling paint, broken fixtures, or degraded materials. The wear should be noticeable and affect the overall impression. Apply when deterioration is more than minor scuffs. Do not use for clean, well maintained interiors. This tag overlaps with visible_dirt/decay but focuses on wear and damage.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the space exhibits visible wear such as scuffs, peeling paint, broken fixtures, or degraded materials. The wear should be noticeable and affect the overall impression. Apply when deterioration is more than minor scuffs. Do not use for clean, well maintained interiors. This tag overlaps with visible_dirt/decay but focuses on wear and damage.
semantics.definition_short: Surfaces or furnishings show wear, damage, or degradation beyond normal use.
examples_negative: A freshly renovated space with intact finishes.
A clean room with no visible damage.
No clear evidence of Worn / Degraded is present.
examples_positive: A hallway with peeling paint and chipped edges.
A room with torn upholstery and broken tiles.
Clear evidence of Worn / Degraded is present.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for visible damage patterns such as chips, cracks, or fraying. Ensure that wear is noticeable across meaningful areas. Distinguish wear from dirt by focusing on physical damage. Avoid tagging if wear is minimal or localized.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use defect detection or material condition metadata to identify damaged areas. Tag when damage coverage exceeds a threshold. Consider both structural surfaces and furniture integrity.
scope_excludes: minor scuffs
clean finishes
well maintained surfaces
scope_includes: peeling paint
chipped surfaces
broken fixtures
torn upholstery

This includes fountains, reflecting pools, indoor water walls, or visible streams. The water should be a visible, designed feature, not incidental moisture. Apply to indoor or outdoor scenes where water is a focal or notable element. Do not use for rain, wet floors, or small containers.
semantics.definition_long: Use when a water feature is clearly present and intended as an architectural or landscape element. This includes fountains, reflecting pools, indoor water walls, or visible streams. The water should be a visible, designed feature, not incidental moisture. Apply to indoor or outdoor scenes where water is a focal or notable element. Do not use for rain, wet floors, or small containers.
semantics.definition_short: Water elements such as fountains, pools, or streams are visible as part of the environment.
examples_negative: A wet floor after cleaning.
A small glass of water on a table.
Metric not computed for Water features.
examples_positive: A lobby with a central fountain and water basin.
A courtyard with a reflecting pool.
Computed metric indicates Water features.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for water surfaces with reflections, ripples, or visible basins. Identify fountain structures, jets, or water walls. Confirm that water is part of the built environment. Avoid false positives from glossy floors or mirrors.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use scene graph objects tagged as water or fountain features. Check material properties such as reflectance and animated surfaces. Tag when water objects occupy a meaningful visible area or are central features.
scope_excludes: rain or puddles
wet surfaces only
small containers of water
scope_includes: fountains
pools and basins
water walls
streams in designed landscapes

env.v2a_070.prospect_refuge_ability_to_s.canonical_name: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen
env.v2a_070.prospect_refuge_ability_to_s.definition: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen; corner refuge availability.

search_terms: Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen

semantics.definition: Use when the scene provides both **prospect** (good outward visibility) and **refuge** (a sense of cover, enclosure, or shielding) such that one could look out without feeling fully exposed. This is an affordance-level tag: it can be implied by geometry and placement of boundaries even if no person is present.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene provides both **prospect** (good outward visibility) and **refuge** (a sense of cover, enclosure, or shielding) such that one could look out without feeling fully exposed. This is an affordance-level tag: it can be implied by geometry and placement of boundaries even if no person is present.
semantics.definition_short: A vantage point affords a clear view outward while the observer is partially concealed or protected.
examples_negative: A completely open plaza with long views but no cover (prospect only).
A small enclosed room with no meaningful outward view (refuge only).
Metric not computed for Prospect–refuge: ability to see without being seen.
examples_positive: A booth along a wall looking across a restaurant with high-backed seating providing cover.
A window seat in an alcove with a view to the street while side walls/overhang provide concealment.
A mezzanine behind a balustrade overlooking a lobby; railing + setback provide partial protection.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Look for partial enclosures around a viewing direction (walls/columns/screens behind or beside the viewpoint) + an open view corridor ahead. In 2D images: strong depth cues (vanishing lines) plus nearby boundary elements framing the viewer position. CV hint: estimate visible depth/line-of-sight corridor; detect nearby vertical boundaries within ~1–3m of camera viewpoint (columns/walls) while distant region remains visible.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Compute isovist / viewshed from candidate occupant positions and combine with refuge metric (nearby occluding surfaces behind/beside). In VR/3D: ray-cast visibility vs proximity-to-cover; flag nodes with high outward visibility + high local enclosure.
scope_excludes: purely open views with no shielding
fully enclosed spaces without outward view
privacy screens that block viewing outward
scope_includes: alcoves, niches, bays with views
overhangs/canopies at overlook points
high-backed seating/booths
balustrades/railings that allow viewing
setbacks behind columns/screens

env.v2a_098.natural_materials_wood_stone.definition: Natural materials: wood/stone; biomorphic forms; nature images.

semantics.definition: Use when the scene features substantial areas of natural wood and stone finishes, giving an organic material character. Both materials should be visible and notable, not just one small accent. Apply when wood and stone are primary materials for floors, walls, or major elements. Do not use when surfaces are synthetic imitations or only one material is present. This tag is about natural material presence.
semantics.definition_long: Use when the scene features substantial areas of natural wood and stone finishes, giving an organic material character. Both materials should be visible and notable, not just one small accent. Apply when wood and stone are primary materials for floors, walls, or major elements. Do not use when surfaces are synthetic imitations or only one material is present. This tag is about natural material presence.
semantics.definition_short: Natural materials such as wood and stone are prominently visible in the space.
examples_negative: A room with wood furniture but no stone surfaces.
A space with stone tile but no visible wood.
Metric not computed for Natural materials: wood / stone.
examples_positive: A lobby with a stone floor and wood wall paneling.
A space with a stone feature wall and timber ceiling beams.
Computed metric indicates Natural materials: wood / stone.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Detect wood grain and stone textures on large surfaces. Confirm presence of both materials within the same scene. Use material classifiers to reduce confusion with printed or synthetic finishes. Avoid tagging if one material is only a small accent.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: Use material tags for wood and stone and compute visible area coverage for each. Tag when both materials exceed minimum coverage thresholds. Validate with surface texture properties and reflectance characteristics.
scope_excludes: synthetic stone look
wood or stone alone
minor accents only
scope_includes: stone walls or floors
wood panels or beams
natural stone and timber finishes
combined wood and stone surfaces

social.approach_invitation.definition: Degree to which entry geometry and seating frontage invite a stranger to initiate contact.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + segmentation (chair, bench, edge, doorway, planter, railing, host_stand) + chair/bench front-vector estimation + entry-geometry parse

key_refs: Whyte, W. H. (1980). The social life of small urban spaces. The Conservation Foundation.
Goffman, E. (1963). Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings. Free Press.
Goffman, E. (1971). Relations in public: Microstudies of the public order. Basic Books.
Oldenburg, R. (1989). The great good place: Cafés, coffee shops, community centers, beauty parlors, general stores, bars, hangouts, and how they get you through the day. Paragon House.
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.

notes.bn_notes: The Spohn 50%-overlap rule is the dominant Engineer-pass concern here. L44 (Sociopetal Seating Topology) shares the chair-orientation observable family with L43, and a naive parent-tag list for both would overlap heavily on `affordance.seating_front_vector_*` family members. To honour the 50% rule, L43's `parent_tags` deliberately *omits* chair-orientation tags as direct parents and instead relies on (a) entry geometry (`threshold_count`, `entry_to_first_seat_distance_m`, `host_barrier_present`), (b) sittable-edge inventory (`sittable_edge_length_m`, `edge_orientation_to_circulation`), and (c) the approach-axis barrier inventory (`barrier_count_at_approach_axis`). The frontage-orientation signal enters the L43 extractor only through the `edge_orientation_to_circulation` tag, which is a circulation-axis-relative measurement, not the chair-pair-relative measurement that L44 will use. Phase D `audit-extraction-plan` should verify zero overlap on chair-pair-relative tags and at most one shared tag on edge tags.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override. V2.6 places L43 at Tier 1 (extractable from a single 2D image) and the Engineer pass concurs: the entry-frontage-barrier triad is recoverable from a single well-framed image with standard segmentation, without recourse to a floor plan or 3D substrate. The two non-extraction notes that *do* require explicit documentation are (a) the Goffman (1971) with/single elision in the V2.6 cue list, flagged in `scope_excludes` and `extraction_notes_2d` and proposed as a Phase F.5 split candidate; and (b) the Hall (1966) Western-calibration warning, flagged in `extraction_notes_2d` per the panel's `cross_cultural_variance: high` assignment. The Spohn 50%-overlap audit against L44 is addressed by parent-tag selection: L43's `bn.parent_tags` deliberately privileges entry-geometry and barrier-inventory tags over chair-orientation tags, leaving L44 the chair-pair-relative observables.

semantics.definition_long: Approach Invitation indexes the extent to which a setting's entry geometry, frontage, and barrier configuration jointly *invite* an unacquainted person — or a group already co-present — to initiate light contact, take a seat near others, or move into the social field of those present. The construct is grounded in Whyte's (1980) observations that "what attracts people most, in sum, is other people" but only when the physical envelope removes the small frictions that otherwise deter approach: turnstiles, walls of backs, raised stages, ambiguous thresholds, missing edges to "perch" on (Whyte, 1980, pp. 19–22). Oldenburg (1989) extends the same logic to "third places," where low approach friction is the constitutive affordance distinguishing a tavern or café from a private club. The construct is *unfocused* in Goffman's (1963) sense — it concerns the conditions of co-presence, not of sustained mutual involvement — and is keyed to the moment *prior to* any focused encounter beginning.
semantics.definition_short: Degree to which entry geometry and seating frontage invite a stranger to initiate contact.
examples_negative: A hotel lobby with deep plush seating arranged in a circle facing inward around a central coffee table — visually warm, but the seated group's backs form a barrier to any newcomer, and the chairs are too low to perch on. (Looks inviting; is closed.)

A trendy café where the entrance opens onto a long communal table parallel to the door — seats are technically available, but every patron sits with their side to the door, no faceable edge, and the host stand interposes. (Open seating, no approach invitation.)
A park with abundant lawn but no edges, walls, or benches; people may walk through but there is no perch from which one can be approached or approach. (High permeability, zero invitation.)

A gallery atrium with a single curved bench in the centre of a polished floor, facing a wall — sittable, but isolated, with no orientation toward circulation; the seated person is *exposed* but not *invitable*.
An outdoor plaza with benches that face away from the main pedestrian flow toward a parking lot — backs to the action, sociofugal in effect even if the chairs are technically present.
examples_positive: A coffee-shop window counter where the row of stools faces outward through a glass front and the doorway sits flush with the sidewalk; a passer-by can see seated patrons and step in without crossing a threshold zone.

A park bench placed perpendicular to a walking path with a clear sightline to the path and no planter or railing intervening, such that a walker may sit at one end without having to step around a barrier.

A library reading-room entrance where the first table sits within four metres of the door, chairs angle toward the entry, and there is no service desk forming a queue-fence between the door and the seats.

A campus quad with low retaining walls at sittable height running along the main diagonal, oriented so seated students face the path; new arrivals can perch beside a seated person without having to "intrude."
A restaurant patio with seating that faces the sidewalk through a low rail (not a hedge or a wall), and a clearly marked entry that opens directly onto the seating area rather than a host stand.
A community-centre lounge whose entrance opens onto an arc of armchairs facing the door, with no reception counter or rope line between the doorway and the chairs.
A market stall where the produce table extends to the aisle edge and the vendor stands behind facing outward, allowing browsers to drift up without crossing a perceived "shop interior" boundary.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: Extraction relies on four upstream observable families, each a distinct Phase D contract. (i) Entry geometry: upstream tags `affordance.threshold_count`, `affordance.entry_to_first_seat_distance_m`, and `affordance.host_barrier_present` parse the doorway-to-seat path and detect interposed reception counters or queue rails; this is the dominant L43 signal and the primary feature distinguishing L43 from sibling L44. (ii) Faceable-edge inventory: upstream tag `affordance.sittable_edge_length_m` (perimeter of low walls, planters, sills at hip-to-knee height) and `affordance.edge_orientation_to_circulation` (parallel vs. perpendicular vs. away). (iii) Frontage orientation: upstream tag `affordance.seating_front_vector_to_path_alignment` (the cosine between mean chair/bench front-vector and the dominant pedestrian axis). (iv) Barrier inventory: upstream tag `affordance.barrier_count_at_approach_axis` (planters, rails, hedges, ropes intersecting the entry-to-seat path). The composite is robust to occlusion of individual seats because the entry-geometry signal carries most of the variance. **Western-calibration warning (Hall, 1966; cross_cultural_variance: high):** the construct as scored here is calibrated against North-European/North-American norms in which face-to-face frontal approach at personal-far distance is the canonical "invitation." Mediterranean settings tolerate closer angles; Japanese settings often substitute side-by-side around a shared focus, in which case the same image will read as low-invitation despite being high-invitation in its own cultural register. Downstream consumers MUST treat the L43 score as culturally indexed. **With/single elision warning (Goffman, 1971, pp. 19–27):** the score does not distinguish invitation extended to a single person from invitation extended to a "with"; a bench that warmly invites a single may actively deter a couple, and vice versa. Phase F.5 should evaluate whether to split L43 into L43a (single-approach) and L43b (with-approach).
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR substrates, the same four upstream families are computed from richer geometric primitives. (i) Entry geometry: full 3D doorway-to-seat path-length and visibility-to-first-seat solid-angle from the entry point, plus host-stand bounding-box detection in the entry corridor. (ii) Faceable-edge inventory: 3D ledge segmentation gives true sittable-edge length at hip-to-knee height (rather than projected length from a single image) and supports the `edge_orientation_to_circulation` measure as a 3D vector dot product. (iii) Frontage orientation: chair/bench front-vectors are recovered directly from object-pose estimation rather than 2D heuristics, giving substantially less noise on the cosine-alignment measure. (iv) Barrier inventory: 3D occluder segmentation (planters, rails, hedges) is straightforward; the approach-axis from any candidate entry point can be queried and intersected against the occluder set. In VR walk-throughs, an additional behavioural proxy is available: time-to-first-seat for a naive avatar approach, which gates the geometric score. None of these 3D extensions changes the construct meaning, only the noise floor of the upstream observables.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators: rating items on "front-facing seating," "inviting entry," "faceable edges," "absence of barriers" load on a single underlying factor whose strongest indicator in pilot data is faceable-edge presence weighted by orientation toward circulation.
scope_excludes: The *with/single distinction* (Goffman, 1971, pp. 19–27): a bench or edge invites a single very differently than it invites a "with" (a couple, family, or already-conversing pair). This V2.6 cue list elides the distinction; L43 measures invitation to approach the setting *as such*, not the differential invitation extended to a single versus a with. A future L43b should split.
The *quality of conversation* once approach has occurred — that is L44 (Sociopetal Seating Topology) and L48–L49 (F-formation arrangement).
The mere availability of seats with no consideration of orientation or barrier configuration — that is closer to L56 (Mingling Affordance).
Cases where invitation is *role-mediated* (a greeter, a host, a doorman) rather than geometrically afforded — that is L46 (Hosting/Service Script).
Approach by an already-acquainted party returning to a known group (this is rejoining, not approaching).
scope_includes: Settings where the entry threshold is geometrically continuous with the social interior (no foyer, no host stand, no queue-fence).
Settings where seating frontage faces the path of approach rather than away from it.
Settings where edges (low walls, wide sills, planter caps) are sittable at hip-to-knee height and run parallel to circulation.
The case of approach by a *single* person (Goffman, 1971, *Relations in Public*, pp. 19–27).

social.boundary_permeability.definition: Ease of entering or leaving a bounded social region, indexed by convex-map depth.

extraction.compute_from: floor-plan-derived convex map (or VR convex partition) + threshold/aperture inventory + normative-control affordance detection

key_refs: Hillier, B., & Hanson, J. (1984). *The social logic of space*. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511597237
Goffman, E. (1963). *Behavior in public places: Notes on the social organization of gatherings*. Free Press.
Whyte, W. H. (1980). *The social life of small urban spaces*. Conservation Foundation.
Hillier, B. (1996). *Space is the machine: A configurational theory of architecture*. Cambridge University Press.

notes.bn_notes: Not required for L54 (ranking-theoretic Sprint-2 flag applies only to L46, L52, L53). One forward-looking note for Phase D: the parent `affordance.convex_depth_to_exterior` is itself a derived observable that requires the floor-plan augmentation pipeline to exist before L54's extractor can be implemented. If that upstream tag is not in the registry by Phase D, L54's `bn.parent_tags` should be marked as `pending_upstream` rather than removed, so the dependency is visible to the BN audit.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override for L54 — Tier 2 is preserved per panel disposition (the override was applied to L41 only). The substantive panel-mandated change for L54 is the addition of `requires_floor_plan: true` and `configurational_measure: depth`, which together signal that pure-image extraction will be unreliable and that the extractor contract must accept floor-plan augmentation. A second panel-mandated discipline operates on `bn.parent_tags`: the Spohn 50%-overlap rule with L42 is enforced by selecting threshold/depth observables for L54 and reserving sightline observables for L42. Engineers implementing the extractor in Sprint 2 should treat the visual surrogates listed in `extraction_notes_2d` as a low-confidence fallback when the floor plan is absent, and should report a confidence interval that explicitly widens in the no-floor-plan case.

semantics.definition_long: Boundary Permeability is the ease with which a person can enter or exit a socially bounded region — a room, an alcove, a courtyard, a plaza interior — without paying a high access cost in either configurational depth, perceptual surveillance, or normative threshold control. Following Hillier and Hanson (1984), the construct is operationalised as *depth in the convex map*: the number of convex-space transitions required to traverse from a defined exterior to the interior in question. Shallower regions (depth 1-2) are highly permeable; deeper regions (depth >= 4) are impermeable in the configurational sense, regardless of how many doors or wide thresholds the photographed boundary itself displays. The convex-map account explains why a sociologically open plaza (Whyte, 1980) and a sociologically closed inner sanctum can look superficially similar in a single image: the difference lives in the global topology of access, not in the local visual openness of a single threshold. Goffman (1963, pp. 151-164) supplies the second leg of the construct — the ritual permission to cross — which colours but does not determine the configurational depth.
semantics.definition_short: Ease of entering or leaving a bounded social region, indexed by convex-map depth.
examples_negative: *Visually open but configurationally deep*: A glass-walled conference room visible across three intervening rooms from the building entrance — looks open in the photograph, but convex-map depth is 4. Permeability is low despite the visual cue.

*Visually closed but configurationally shallow*: A small reading nook entered through a single narrow wood door directly off the main lobby — depth 1, but the visible 'closed door' cue would misclassify it as impermeable.

*High exterior visibility, normatively gated*: An office reception fronted by floor-to-ceiling glass directly off the street, but the door is keycard-locked and a turnstile sits behind it. The convex transition exists but is normatively impermeable.

*Permeable threshold, surveilled interior*: A courtyard entered through a wide unlocked gate, but with a security desk visible from the gate that issues badges to all entrants. Permeable in geometry, gated in social practice.

*Inverse case for L42 distinction*: A reading room with full sightlines from the corridor (high interactional visibility) but reached only via a stair to a third floor and then through two intervening rooms — the visibility is high, the permeability is low.
examples_positive: A ground-floor café whose seating area is one convex step from the public sidewalk through a wide retracted door — depth 1, no normative gating, high permeability.
A plaza with multiple low-curb entrances on three sides, where any pedestrian on any adjacent street can step into the interior in a single transition.
A hotel lobby whose lounge area is reached from the porte-cochère through a single open archway — convex depth 2, no door swing, no reception barrier between exterior and lounge.
A library reading room whose entry is a wide arched opening from a flanking corridor that itself opens directly to the foyer — total convex depth 3 with no doors at any transition.
A market hall arcade whose stalls are accessible from the surrounding street through any of six unmanned arched openings, with partial knee-wall partitions but no full enclosure.
An open-plan museum gallery reachable from the main hall via a single threshold-less floor change — depth 1 with only material articulation marking the boundary.
A university quad with four diagonal paths converging on a central lawn, each path entering through a gap in low hedging — multiple shallow entrances, no normative gating.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A single image is insufficient to score L54 reliably; the panel-mandated `requires_floor_plan: true` flag governs. Where only an image is available, the extractor must combine local cues with floor-plan augmentation. Local upstream observables expected: `affordance.threshold_count` (number of distinct apertures visible at the boundary of the region), `affordance.threshold_aperture_width` (clear-width estimate per aperture), `affordance.door_state` (open / partial / closed / absent), `affordance.barrier_height` (knee-wall vs. waist-wall vs. full partition), and `affordance.normative_control_present` (security desk, host stand, ticket booth, turnstile, locked-hardware indicator). These cues are *visual surrogates* for the underlying convex-map depth and will systematically misclassify the two failure modes documented in `examples_negative` 1 and 2: visually open but configurationally deep, and visually closed but configurationally shallow. The extractor must therefore lower its confidence sharply when the floor plan is absent and the local cues are ambiguous. Critical Spohn-rule note: L54's `bn.parent_tags` deliberately exclude pure sightline observables (e.g. `affordance.isovist_area`, `affordance.transparent_surface_fraction`), which belong to L42 — overlap of more than 50% with L42 will trigger the `audit-extraction-plan` validator.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: With a 3D model or VR walkthrough, the convex partition can be computed directly: segment the navigable floor surface into maximal convex regions, build the adjacency graph, and compute geodesic depth from each exterior-flagged region to each interior region of interest. The depth value, mapped through a monotonic compression (e.g. depth 1 -> 4, depth 2 -> 3, depth 3 -> 2, depth 4 -> 1, depth >= 5 -> 0), yields the geometric component of the Likert score. Connectivity (number of distinct shortest paths of equal depth) provides the multi-entrance bonus. Normative-control affordances detected during the walkthrough (turnstiles, manned reception, badge readers) are applied as a multiplicative discount before final ordinal binning. When only monocular-3D approximation is available, depth estimation degrades to 'partial' because depth-from-defocus and monocular-depth nets recover *visible* depth but cannot recover the topology of unseen rooms beyond the photographed boundary; this is why `monocular_3d_approx` is set to `partial` and not `yes` despite the underlying construct being natively geometric.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet for L54 expected factor structure: a single dominant factor loading on (a) number of convex transitions to interior, (b) presence/absence of physical barriers per transition, (c) presence/absence of normative control at any transition, (d) number of distinct entry points. Behavioural proxies (observed flow rate, observed entry/exit count) are reserved for extractor validation, not registry-level content.
scope_excludes: *Sightline openness alone* — visual transparency to a region from outside is the province of L42 (Interactional Visibility), not L54. A glass-walled but configurationally deep room is L42-high, L54-low.

*Density of foot traffic actually observed* in the image — that is downstream evidence about realised use (closer to L41, Chance-Encounter Potential), not about the boundary's permeability as a structural property.
*Door-swing direction or hardware* per se — a heavy door is no less a single convex transition than a light one; the construct lives at the convex-map level, not the affordance-detail level.
*Exit affordance under emergency conditions* — life-safety egress is governed by code requirements that are largely orthogonal to the social-encounter construct of permeability.
scope_includes: Configurational depth on a convex map computed from the floor plan, including outdoor convex spaces (plazas, courtyards) when the plan is drawn at appropriate scale.

Normative boundary control (security desks, hosts, ticket booths, badge readers) as a qualifier that *reduces* permeability without changing convex depth — encoded as a downward adjustment in the Likert score.
Multi-entrance topologies where a single region has more than one convex link to the exterior — these *increase* permeability, captured by the convex map's connectivity along with depth.

social.chance_encounter_potential.definition: Likelihood the spatial layout produces unplanned co-presence among inhabitants.

extraction.compute_from: floor-plan-derived axial map + global integration value (Rn or R3) for the depicted region; image used only for region localisation

key_refs: Hillier, B. (1996). Space is the machine: A configurational theory of architecture. Cambridge University Press.
Hillier, B., & Hanson, J. (1984). The social logic of space. Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511597237
Whyte, W. H. (1980). The social life of small urban spaces. Conservation Foundation.

notes.bn_notes: (Optional for L41 — no Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding flag required.) Note for downstream BN integration: because `configuration.axial_integration_global` is itself a precomputed configurational measure rather than a direct image observable, the conditional probability table for L41 will be unusually peaked on that single parent. Sprint 2 should consider whether to model L41 as deterministic-given-integration (with the binning thresholds as the only free parameters) rather than as a full CPT — this matches the natural-movement theorem's implication that integration *is* the substrate, not merely a correlate.
notes.extraction_notes: **V2.6 Tier override (panel-mandated, 28 April 2026):** V2.6 assigns L41 to Tier 2 ("partial from 2D"). The Sprint 1 architectural panel demotes L41 to Tier 3 ("not extractable from 2D, 3D, or monocular-3D approximation alone") on Hillier's argument. Per Hillier (1996, ch. 4), chance-encounter potential is governed by the natural-movement theorem: encounter rates are a function of through-movement, through-movement is a function of integration in the axial map, and the axial map is a property of the *whole* configuration — invisible in any single image regardless of richness. The override is documented in `docs/sprint1_panel_disposition_2026-04-28.md` §3(ii) and is one of the four construct contests deliberately surfaced for the student handoff (`LATENT_INTEGRATION_NOTES_2026-05-04.md`, contest #1). Students may relitigate this override in Week 5; the panel's position is recorded here as the registry's commitment, not as a closed question.
