To stay under the 50% overlap bound, L53's parent set is specified to use `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` and `pose.head_yaw_field` (gaze observables) rather than the generic `furniture.chair_orientation_field` parent that L52 uses, even though the underlying upstream signal is related. Phase D `audit-extraction-plan` will verify the disjointness of these parent sets; if the 50%-overlap test fails, the resolution is to further decompose `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` into anchor-direction-specific observables that L52 does not consume. A second potential overlap is with the F-formation cluster (L48 / L49), which shares orientation observables. The disambiguating parent is `affordance.focal_object_present`: F-formations specify the o-space's geometric arrangement without requiring a materially-marked anchor within it, while L53 requires both. Audit should treat the F-formation cluster's lack of an `affordance.focal_object_*` parent as the structural distinguisher.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied. V2.6 Tier 1 is preserved: the construct is genuinely image-extractable because both poles of the triad — the focal anchor and the seating-orientation convergence — are visible in a single still image without floor-plan augmentation or whole-system configurational measures. The construct is binary at first approximation and is encoded as `latent_score` (binary) per the panel disposition (sec.3 (x)). Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding is flagged in `bn_notes`. The panel-mandated triadic-structure exclusion (Kendon's argument that V2.6's 'dominant focal object/wall' cue identifies a *candidate* anchor but not the triadic structure that joint attention requires) is operationalised in the extractor via the orientation-convergence criterion (>= 60% of seating yaw vectors within +/-35 degrees of the anchor-direction vector). This is the most consequential extraction decision for L53: it commits the registry to coding *anchor-without-orientation* configurations (a striking wall in a lobby whose seating ignores it) as L53-negative even though the V2.6 cue language treats them as positive instances.

semantics.definition_long: A *shared attention anchor* is the spatial substrate of the joint-attention triad — the configuration in which a salient environmental feature (a focal object, wall, display, screen, hearth, central table feature) is paired with seating, postural, or circulation orientations that converge on that feature, jointly fixing a stable shared focus for the gathering. The construct is binary at first approximation: either the room commits to a single, materially-marked anchor that the seating arrangement actively orients toward, or it does not. The triadic specification is constitutive, not optional. Following Tomasello (1995, 2008), joint attention requires the simultaneous coordination of self, other, and a shared object of attention; the spatial-registry analog therefore requires both (i) a perceptually dominant anchor and (ii) a seating or body-axis arrangement that orients the participants' gaze and posture toward it. Following Kendon (1990, ch. 7), the anchor functions as the shared *focus* of the o-space — the inner transactional region of an F-formation — and a focal feature without an o-space oriented toward it is not a shared attention anchor but merely a salient object. Following Goffman (1963, ch. 6), a focused gathering is constituted by the participants' coordinated involvement in a single cognitive and visual concern; the anchor is the material correlate of that concern. L53 thus differs from L52 (one-to-many presentation) in that L52 specifies the *participation framework* (asymmetric speaker/audience) whereas L53 specifies whether a *triadic anchor* exists — a hearth-and-circle, a central conference table feature, or a museum display with a viewing bench all qualify for L53 without being one-to-many presentations.
semantics.definition_short: Triadic anchor: salient focal object plus seating oriented toward it, fixing shared focus.
examples_negative: A blank wall in a hotel lobby with a single piece of large abstract art mounted on it, but the lobby seating is arranged around small coffee tables with seats facing each other rather than the wall — the focal object is present but no triadic anchor exists. (Per the panel: focal object without seating oriented toward it is mere object presence, not a shared attention anchor.)

A fast-food restaurant with a wall-mounted television playing news, but tables and chairs are arranged for paired or quartet conversation with seats facing across the table; the television is salient but the seating refuses it as an anchor.

A hotel lobby with multiple competing focal candidates — a reception desk, a grand staircase, a video wall, a fountain — and dispersed seating clusters that orient toward different candidates or toward each other; no single shared anchor is established.
An airport gate area with rows of perimeter seating facing a central empty floor; there is no central feature and the seating axis converges on nothing.

A bare meeting room with chairs arranged in a circle around an empty conversation centre; the o-space exists (sociopetal seating) but there is no materially-marked anchor occupying it — this is L48/L49 territory, not L53.
examples_positive: A living room with a fireplace and a U-shaped sofa-and-armchair arrangement whose seating axes all converge on the hearth — the fireplace is the materially-marked anchor and the seating commits the room to it.
A museum gallery with a single sculpture on a central plinth and a curved bench arc placed at the canonical viewing distance facing the plinth.

A boardroom with an oblong conference table whose centre carries a fixed visual feature (a built-in display, an architectural model, a centrepiece) and chairs arrayed around the table with their long axes oriented toward that centre.
A campfire-style outdoor gathering: a fire ring at the centre, log benches arranged in a ring, every body axis oriented inward and downward toward the flames.

A waiting area in a children's hospital with a fish tank along one wall and a row of seats positioned and angled to face the tank, such that the tank functions as a shared point of regard for waiting families.
A classroom corner reading nook organised around a low display shelf of picture books, with floor cushions arrayed in a semicircle facing the shelf.

A traditional Japanese tea room with a *tokonoma* alcove containing a hanging scroll and arranged flowers; the seating positions for guests are fixed by convention to face the alcove, making it the canonical shared focus.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: A 2D image is sufficient when upstream observables jointly identify (a) a single dominant anchor candidate AND (b) seating whose orientation axes converge on that candidate. The extractor should consume upstream tags `affordance.focal_object_present` (binary on whether a perceptually dominant non-seating feature exists — fireplace, sculpture, screen, central table feature, display wall), `affordance.focal_object_saliency_score` (a continuous saliency or visual-prominence score for the top-ranked candidate, used to discriminate single-anchor from competing-anchor scenes), `affordance.focal_object_polygon` (region support for the anchor itself), `furniture.chair_orientation_field` (per-chair yaw vector), `furniture.seating_centroid` (the geometric centre of the seated region), and `geometry.anchor_to_seating_axis_angle` (the angle between the seating-orientation principal direction and the anchor-to-centroid vector). The binary positive judgement requires (i) `affordance.focal_object_present == true`, (ii) `affordance.focal_object_saliency_score` above a single-anchor discrimination threshold (i.e. one anchor visually dominates rather than competing with peers), AND (iii) >= 60% of detected seating yaw vectors falling within +/-35 degrees of the anchor-direction vector from the seating centroid. The orientation-convergence criterion is the operationalisation of the panel-mandated triadic-structure requirement: a focal wall *with* seats facing it constitutes a triad; a focal wall *with seats facing each other* does not, and must be coded negative even when the anchor candidate is visually unambiguous. Because L53 co-occurs with L52 in most presentation contexts, the extractor should NOT short-circuit on L52-positive scenes; instead it should re-verify the anchor-orientation criterion independently, since some L52-positive scenes (a lectern in an empty room, a podium with no backdrop) lack the L53 anchor object even when the participation framework is committed. Cross-cultural variance is medium: the anchor-and-orientation pattern recurs widely (hearths, altars, *tokonoma* alcoves, display niches all exemplify it), but cultures vary in *what kinds of objects* qualify as canonical anchors and in *how strongly* the seating commitment is materially marked versus conventionally enforced. Extractors trained on Western interiors may underweight non-Western anchor types (alcoves, low altars, ground-level focal arrangements) and should be audited cross-culturally before deployment.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In 3D / VR scans the same upstream observables are available with stronger geometric grounding. Anchor-candidate detection benefits from full 3D saliency: a fireplace, sculpture, or display zone that is partially occluded in a 2D image is fully recoverable from mesh geometry. Per-chair yaw vectors are recoverable from chair-back normal vectors rather than monocular pose estimation, and the orientation-convergence criterion can be evaluated by testing whether the union of chair viewing frusta intersects coherently within the bounding region of the anchor polygon. The 3D extractor should compute (i) the saliency-ranked anchor candidate set within the visible mesh (using a combination of geometric prominence — surface-area exposure, central placement — and material-marker cues such as luminance or material discontinuity), (ii) per-chair viewing direction from chair-back normals, (iii) the intersection point or region of densest intersection of those view rays, and (iv) whether that intersection region overlaps the top-ranked anchor candidate's bounding region. A 3D positive instance requires the orientation-intersection region to fall within (or immediately adjacent to) the anchor-candidate region. Monocular-3D-approximation extraction is also viable (Tier 1 in V2.6) using monocular depth estimation plus a planar-floor assumption; the binary judgement is robust to depth noise because it depends on the *direction* of seating-axis convergence relative to the anchor rather than absolute distances. Region support is true: positive instances should return both the anchor polygon and the seated-region polygon, allowing downstream consumers to verify the triad geometry. The 3D extractor should also emit (alongside the binary value) the diagnostic triple `(anchor_polygon, seating_centroid, mean_axis_misalignment_degrees)` so that borderline cases can be inspected in Sprint 2 calibration.
factor_associations: V2.6 *Latent_Indicators* sheet, factor 'Shared Focus Anchor': rating items for (a) presence of a perceptually dominant focal object/wall/display, (b) presence of a central table feature, (c) presence of a designated display zone, (d) seating orientation convergence on the candidate anchor. The fourth indicator — orientation convergence — is the operationalisation of the triadic-structure requirement. Behavioural proxies (people actually looking at the anchor) belong in extractor docstrings, not the registry.
scope_excludes: Focal object without seating oriented toward it. Mere object presence — a striking wall, a large artwork, a display screen — does not constitute a shared attention anchor unless the seating arrangement actively orients toward it. (Panel-mandated exclusion: per Kendon, the V2.6 cue 'dominant focal object/wall' identifies a candidate anchor but not the triadic structure that joint attention requires. A focal wall with seats facing it constitutes a triad; a focal wall with seats facing each other does not.)

Sociopetal seating without a materially-marked anchor. A circle of chairs around an empty centre is an F-formation o-space (L48 / L49 / L51) but lacks the object pole of the joint-attention triad and is therefore not L53.

Multi-anchor or competing-anchor configurations. Rooms with several plausible focal candidates and dispersed orientations do not commit to a single shared focus and should be coded negative on L53 even if individual sub-clusters within the room would qualify on their own.

Pure platform-format configurations where the anchor is the speaker rather than an environmental feature. L52 captures the speaker-and-audience commitment as a *participation framework*; L53 captures the *environmental object* pole of the triad. A bare lectern with no displayed content or backdrop, in a room committed to one-to-many seating, satisfies L52 without satisfying L53. (In practice, most lecture halls satisfy both — the screen, board, or framed front region acts as the L53 anchor while the speaker animates the L52 framework.)

Transient or contingent foci (a person being looked at, a momentary event) that depend on participant behaviour rather than the room's spatial commitments. The registry codes affordances for joint attention, not behavioural episodes of joint attention.
scope_includes: Domestic hearth-and-seating arrangements (fireplaces, central low tables, family altars) where the anchor is a permanent architectural or furniture feature and the surrounding seating commits the room to it.

Display-zone configurations (museum benches before sculpture, gallery viewing seats before paintings, observatory seating before a window or telescope) where the anchor is curatorially designated and seating is calibrated to it.

One-to-many presentation rooms (lecture halls, classrooms) where L52 *and* L53 both apply: the front region is also a shared attention anchor, and the audience seating orientation simultaneously satisfies both constructs. (L53 is not exclusive of L52; the constructs co-occur frequently. See `related_tags`.)

Central-table configurations whose table-top itself carries a focal feature (architectural model, game board, candelabrum, food platter at a banquet) that anchors the gaze of the seated participants inward and downward.

social.small_group_conversation.definition: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.
social.small_group_conversation.definition_short: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + chair-instance segmentation + per-chair orientation + chair-cluster grouping + round-table detection + free-floor pocket estimation + monocular distance estimate + ambient-acoustic-context proxy

key_refs: Kendon, A. (1976). The F-formation system: The spatial organization of social encounters. *Man-Environment Systems*, *6*(6), 291–296. (Foundational F-formation article; pre-DOI era.)

Kendon, A. (1990). *Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters*. Cambridge University Press. (Definitive treatment of p-space and o-space; ch. 7 on F-formation arrangements; pp. 209–214 on the analytic priority of the o-space.)

Hall, E. T. (1966). *The hidden dimension*. Doubleday. (Personal/social distance bands; ch. 10 on cross-cultural variance; the metric basis for distinguishing L49 conversational distance from L51 seminar distance.)

Steiner, I. D. (1972). *Group process and productivity*. Academic Press. (Small-group size effects; the 3–6 band as the size at which a single sustained conversation remains tractable without sub-grouping; ch. 4.)

Setti, F., Hung, H., & Cristani, M. (2013). Group detection in still images by F-formation modeling: A comparative study. In *Proceedings of the 14th International Workshop on Image Analysis for Multimedia Interactive Services (WIAMIS)* (pp. 1–4). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/WIAMIS.2013.6616147

notes.bn_notes: Not required for L49 (Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding flag applies only to L46, L52, L53). However: L49's secondary-Likert affordance-strength score depends on three multiplicatively-related sub-judgments (size-fit, free-floor adequacy, acoustic tractability), and the BN's CPT for L49 may be more parsimoniously expressed as a noisy-AND of three intermediate latent nodes than as a single 5-state CPT over the full parent set. This is a Sprint-2 BN-modelling refinement to consider when L49 is integrated into the network.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied — L49 remains Tier 1 as in V2.6. Three extractor-contract requirements deserve special note for the Phase E stub author: (a) the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` is mandatory output (Kendon panel mandate, panel disposition §3 ix); (b) the Hall distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is mandatory output (Hall panel mandate, panel disposition §3 viii) — and for L49 this is doubly load-bearing because without metric the construct collapses into L51 per the panel's special note for this latent; (c) the acoustic-tractability proxy (the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue) should be emitted as a separate boolean alongside the secondary Likert score so that downstream consumers can re-weight or veto on acoustic grounds. All three are runtime extractor-output specifications, not registry-level fields, and so are recorded here rather than in `extractability`.

semantics.definition_long: The property of a local spatial pocket whose furniture clustering, free-floor geometry, and ambient conditions afford the formation and maintenance of a focused conversational gathering of roughly three to six co-present persons — Steiner's (1972) small-group band, in which mutual perceptual access remains tractable without sub-grouping. The construct refers to a *cluster-scale* F-formation arrangement in Kendon's (1990, ch. 7) sense: a shared o-space large enough to seat or stand-circle three to six people at conversational distance, enclosed by clusterable furniture (movable chairs, round tables of suitable diameter, an open floor pocket), and acoustically tractable enough that ordinary speech across the o-space remains intelligible. L49 is distinct from L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), which is a 2-person F-formation construct, and from L51 (Group-Assembly Affordance), which addresses 7+ gatherings whose geometry must distribute people into sub-groups; the 3–6 band is the size at which one o-space still suffices and one conversation can still be sustained (Hall, 1966, ch. 10; Steiner, 1972, ch. 4).
semantics.definition_short: Spatial support for a focused 3–6 person F-formation, standing or seated.
examples_negative: A long rectangular boardroom table seating twelve, with conference chairs along both sides — the geometry is sized for a single 7+ gathering with formal facing-across, not for a 3–6 person F-formation; this is a presentation/large-group configuration (L51 / L52), not L49.
A pair of armchairs angled inward across a small coffee table in an alcove — the cluster is sized only for two; this is L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), not L49.

A row of single fixed seats along a corridor wall, all facing the same direction — there is no clusterable floor pocket and no convergent o-space; this is at most a peripheral-engagement / waiting-area arrangement, not L49.
A theatre-style seating block with five chairs in a single row, all facing forward — the seat count is right but the geometry is parallel-rank with no o-space; this is L52 (presentation), not L49.

A loud open-plan office break area where four chairs sit around a small table but the ambient noise from adjacent desks and equipment makes ordinary conversation across the table impossible — the geometry says L49 but the acoustic-tractability criterion fails; classify as `none` with a low secondary affordance score and an acoustic-context note.

A bar with seven movable stools spaced along a straight counter-front; the seats are clusterable in count, but the linear-rank geometry and the wide spacing do not enclose an o-space for 3–6 people, so the construct does not apply.
examples_positive: A round café table about 110 cm in diameter ringed by four movable chairs in a coffee-shop alcove; chair backs face outward, chair fronts converge on the table, and the surrounding open floor is wide enough for a fifth person to pull a chair up.

A clearing in a hotel-lobby furniture plan with a small low coffee table and five lounge chairs roughly evenly spaced around it at conversational distance, with no other furniture obstructing the cluster.

A standing huddle pocket in an exhibition hall where a high cocktail table is surrounded by an open floor disk roughly 2.5 m across, allowing four to six adults to stand-circle the table without bumping passers-by.

A meeting-room "informal corner" with three movable tub chairs and a sofa seat for one more, all converging on a small round table; the corner is set off from the main meeting table and supports a 3–4 person side-discussion.

A library group-study nook with a round table seating up to six, movable chairs, glass walls for acoustic separation, and an open pocket of floor that lets group members swap seats without disturbing the cluster.
A picnic ground arrangement of one round picnic table seating six, with the surrounding lawn open enough that group members can stand and lean during conversation while remaining in the same o-space.
A workplace "team huddle" zone with a round high-top table for four to six standing people, surrounded by clear floor and acoustically buffered from the open-plan desks beyond.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observable tags — most centrally `furniture.chair_cluster_count` (number of chairs spatially grouped within an inter-chair distance threshold), `furniture.round_table_present` (round-table detection within the cluster), `furniture.chair_orientation` (per-chair front-normal in image plane), `geometry.free_floor_pocket_radius` (distance from cluster centroid to the nearest occlusion or through-traffic boundary), `geometry.cluster_distance_estimate` (Hall-zone distance estimate between cluster members; mandated by Hall-panel disposition §3 viii because without metric, L49 collapses into L51 — a circle of chairs at social-close distance (4–7 ft / 1.2–2.1 m centres) supports L49 conversation, while the same chairs at social-far (7–12 ft / 2.1–3.7 m) supports a seminar (L51 / L52)), and `scene.ambient_noise_proxy` (a visual proxy for the acoustic context: presence of acoustic baffling, distance from open kitchen / equipment, glass-wall enclosure, etc.) for the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue. The extractor MUST emit, alongside the categorical and secondary-Likert values, the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` per Kendon-panel mandate (panel disposition §3 ix; Kendon, 1990, pp. 209–214). Cluster-size gating: the categorical value defaults to `none` when the chair-cluster-count is <3 (cede to L48) or >6 (cede to L51); within the 3–6 band, the `predominant_arrangement` is computed from the same angular-threshold logic as L44 (vis-à-vis at ~180° front-normal, L at ~90°, side-by-side at ~0° with shared focal artefact, semicircular at chair-back arc <180° around the centroid, circular at arc ≥270°). **Cross-cultural-variance warning (Hall, 1966, ch. 10)**: this extractor is calibrated against Western indoor-furniture corpora; it will systematically misread (a) Japanese tatami-room small-group arrangements where seating is around a low shared table at distances Western thresholds may flag as too close, (b) Mediterranean / Latin-American small-group conversational distances which the Hall-zone gate may push down a Likert step, and (c) Middle-Eastern majlis-style perimeter seating where the ring is technically circular but at distances exceeding the o-space radius constraint. Downstream model trainers MUST treat L49 outputs as culturally indexed and the secondary Likert as conditionally interpretable on the cultural prior of the scene.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction is materially easier: chair instances and orientations are metric, the floor plane is given, and the free-floor pocket can be computed as the largest empty disk centred on the cluster centroid that contains no other furniture or wall surface up to 0.9 m height. The extractor should take the 3D scene graph, identify chair clusters whose pairwise floor-plane distance falls within the Hall personal-far / social-close band (1.2–2.5 m centre-to-centre — the metric that distinguishes L49 from L51 per Hall, 1966, ch. 10, and per the panel's special note that without distance estimation L49 collapses into L51), gate on cluster size 3 ≤ n ≤ 6, project chair-front normals onto the floor plane, and compute the o-space centroid as the smallest enclosed region whose boundary contains all chair-front rays within their first 2.5 m. The arrangement-enum classification applies the same angular thresholds as in 2D but with metric distance gating: a circle of six chairs at 1.4 m centres is `circular` with secondary score 4; the same chairs at 3 m centres steps down to secondary 2 (and at 4 m centres steps to `none` because the cluster has crossed into seminar-scale L51 territory). Acoustic context is more directly estimable in 3D from room volume, surface materials, and distance to noise sources; the secondary score should integrate a simple acoustic-tractability boolean from these. As with L44 and L48, occupancy state is not part of L49 — the construct is an *affordance* of the geometry alone, with avatar occupancy contributing only to a separate confidence-of-realisation field. The `requires_floor_plan: false` setting holds even in 3D: L49 is a local cluster-pocket property, not a configurational property of the room as a whole.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CONV-3 (Small-Group Conversation Affordance): rating items "the space supports a small group talking", "movable chairs around a round table are present", "the floor pocket is open enough for 3–6 people to gather"; behavioral proxy "if 3–6 people stood/sat here, they could carry on one conversation".

Loads on the V2.6 *interactional-affordance* superfactor alongside L44 (Sociopetal Seating Topology), L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), and L51 (Group-Assembly Affordance), with L49 occupying the 3–6 person mid-band specifically.
scope_excludes: Dyadic clusters (two seats only, however well-arranged) — these belong to L48 (Dyadic Intimacy).
Large-group arrangements sized for 7+ persons whose geometry forces sub-grouping into multiple simultaneous conversations — these belong to L51 (Group Assembly Affordance).

Single-cluster geometry that meets the 3–6 size criterion but sits in an environment where the ambient noise floor exceeds normal-speech intelligibility across the o-space (the V2.6 "not too loud-looking" cue) — record as `none` with a noted acoustic-tractability failure rather than a positive `circular`/`semicircular` value.
One-to-many presentation arrangements where 3–6 chairs all face one focal speaker (lectern, screen, performer) rather than converging on a shared inner o-space — these are L52 (presentation).

Sociofugal back-to-back or radial-out clusters whose seats are within range numerically (3–6) but whose front-normals diverge — Osmond's (1957) sociofugal pole, the inverse of the construct, recorded as `none` with secondary score 0.
scope_includes: Multi-element clusters (chairs, round table, standing-table, soft seating mix) whose combined enclosed o-space is sized to seat or stand-circle 3 to 6 persons at conversational distance (Hall's (1966) personal-far to social-close band, roughly 1.2–2.5 m centre-to-centre).

Standing-only huddle arrangements (no seats, only a high table or nothing at all) provided the surrounding floor pocket is open, free of through-traffic, and acoustically buffered enough for a stand-circle to form.
Mixed-furniture clusters (e.g., one sofa segment plus three pull-up chairs) where the combined seat count falls in the 3–6 band and the chair fronts converge on a shared table or floor centroid.

Round-table arrangements at the upper boundary (six seats) where a single shared conversation can plausibly be sustained without splitting into sub-groups; the upper edge is fuzzy and depends on table diameter and local acoustics.

semantics.definition: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_long: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data. Use when this attribute is clearly present and salient in the scene or data. Do not infer without evidence.
semantics.definition_short: Tag indicating presence of Sociopetal in the scene or data.
examples_negative: Scene does not show Sociopetal.
Sociopetal is not visibly present.

social.sociopetal_seating.definition: Seating geometry whose angular convergence affords sustained face-to-face talk (F-formations).

extraction.compute_from: 2D image + chair-instance segmentation + per-chair orientation estimate + pairwise chair-front-normal angular geometry + optional intervening-artefact (table/hearth) detection

key_refs: Osmond, H. (1957). Function as the basis of psychiatric ward design. Mental Hospitals, 8(4), 23–29.
Hall, E. T. (1966). The hidden dimension. Doubleday.
Kendon, A. (1976). The F-formation system: The spatial organization of social encounters. Man-Environment Systems, 6(6), 291–296.
Kendon, A. (1990). Conducting interaction: Patterns of behavior in focused encounters. Cambridge University Press.

Setti, F., Hung, H., & Cristani, M. (2013). Group detection in still images by F-formation modeling: A comparative study. In Proceedings of the 14th International Workshop on Image Analysis for Multimedia Interactive Services (WIAMIS) (pp. 1–4). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/WIAMIS.2013.6616147

notes.bn_notes: Not required for L44 (Sprint-2 ranking-theoretic recoding flag applies only to L46, L52, L53). However: the Kendon-panel ontological argument that L52 is itself an F-formation variant (panel disposition §3 xii, deferred) suggests that if Sprint 2 re-opens that question, the L44 categorical enum may need to grow a `parallel_rank` value to absorb L52, or alternatively L52 may be re-keyed as a special case of L44 with the `none` enum value flagged with a presentation-arrangement subtype. This is logged as a Sprint-2 construct-contest item for the student handoff.
notes.extraction_notes: No V2.6 Tier override applied — L44 remains Tier 1 as in V2.6. Two extractor-contract requirements deserve special note for the Phase E stub author: (a) the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` is mandatory output (Kendon panel mandate, panel disposition §3 ix); (b) the Hall distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is mandatory output (Hall panel mandate, panel disposition §3 viii). Both are runtime extractor-output specifications, not registry-level fields, and so are recorded here rather than in `extractability`.

semantics.definition_long: The spatial-organisational property of a multi-seat arrangement whose chair positions and orientations enclose a shared inner void — Kendon's *o-space* — such that two or more co-present persons, were they to occupy the seats, would form a transactional segment supporting sustained mutual orientation, gaze, and talk (Kendon, 1990, ch. 7). The construct is Osmond's (1957) "sociopetal" pole — arrangements that *bring people together* for conversation — operationalised through the F-formation tradition's distinction between p-space (the slot the bodies and chairs occupy) and o-space (the shared inner region around which the bodies are oriented). It is a property of *arrangements of multiple seats*, not of single chairs; a single chair, however oriented, cannot constitute a sociopetal topology because no o-space is enclosed.
semantics.definition_short: Seating geometry whose angular convergence affords sustained face-to-face talk (F-formations).
examples_negative: A row of theatre seats all facing forward toward a stage — each seat is occupied for focused attention, but the seats face *parallel*, not convergent, so no inter-seat o-space is enclosed; this is a one-to-many presentation arrangement (L52), not sociopetal.

A single armchair angled toward an open doorway in a hallway — looks "inviting" because of orientation, but no second seat is present to enclose an o-space; this is at most an approach-invitation cue (L43), not a sociopetal topology.
Four airport-terminal chairs welded back-to-back in a cross pattern with all chair-fronts pointing radially *outward* — Osmond's classic sociofugal configuration; the inverse construct.
A long boardroom table with chairs only along one side, all facing a wall-mounted screen — chairs face a common focus but do not face *each other*, so no F-formation o-space is enclosed.

A pair of chairs side-by-side on a porch, both facing the same garden view — Hall (1966) notes that side-by-side parallel seating affords companionship but not the mutual gaze of vis-à-vis sociopetal seating; classification depends on whether a shared o-space (e.g., a small table between them) is enclosed (see scope_includes 3).
A single sofa with three cushions, all facing the same TV — the cushions are clustered but their fronts are coplanar; no convergence, no o-space.
examples_positive: A pair of armchairs angled inward at roughly 90° across a small coffee table in a hotel lobby; the chair fronts converge on a shared point above the table surface, enclosing a clear o-space.
Four café chairs around a small round table, each chair-front normal pointing approximately at the table centre; the inward-facing seat ring defines a circular F-formation.
A semicircular cluster of low lounge chairs around a fireplace in a reading room, all chair backs on the convex side and chair fronts converging on the hearth-as-shared-focus.

A pair of bench seats in a railway compartment facing each other across a fold-down table, the backs flush with opposite walls and the o-space the table-top void between them — a vis-à-vis arrangement.
Three chairs in an L-arrangement at the corner of a sectional sofa, two side-by-side and one perpendicular, all three chair-fronts converging on a coffee-table o-space.
A circle of chairs in a therapy-group room with no central table, the o-space defined purely by the convergence of chair-front normals on the empty floor centre.
A pair of bar stools turned inward toward each other across the corner of an L-shaped bar counter, fronts converging at the corner.
semantics.extraction_notes_2d: The extractor consumes upstream observable tags — most centrally `furniture.chair_instances` (per-chair bounding box and instance mask), `furniture.chair_orientation` (per-chair front-normal vector in image plane, or its monocular 3D lift), `furniture.table_instances` and `furniture.hearth_instances` (intervening-artefact candidates), and `scene.floor_plane_normal` (for projecting chair fronts onto the floor plane to get planar angular geometry). The Setti et al. (2013) detection pipeline is the closest published precedent: detect upper-body or chair-front orientations, then cluster orientation lines by their intersection points to identify candidate F-formation o-spaces. For L44 specifically, the extractor MUST emit, alongside the categorical and secondary-Likert values, the F-formation-geometry sub-field `(o_space_centroid_xy, o_space_radius_px, predominant_arrangement)` per Kendon-panel mandate (Kendon, 1990, pp. 209–214); the V2.6 cue list "seats facing each other; circular/semicircular arrangements; clustered chairs" describes only the p-space furniture and elides the o-space, which is the analytically-load-bearing region. Predominant-arrangement classification proceeds: 2 chairs at front-normal angle ~180° → `vis_a_vis`; 2 chairs at ~90° → `l_arrangement`; 2+ chairs at ~0° (parallel) with shared focal artefact → `side_by_side`; 3+ chairs whose front-normals converge on a common centroid with chair-back arc spanning <180° of the centroid → `semicircular`; same with arc spanning ≥270° → `circular`; otherwise → `none`. Distance-zone estimate per Hall (1966) is required as a separate extractor-output field (panel disposition §3 viii). **Cross-cultural-variance warning (Hall, 1966, ch. 10)**: this extractor is calibrated against Western indoor-furniture corpora; it will systematically misread (a) Japanese tea-room and tatami arrangements where side-by-side around a shared focus substitutes for face-to-face, (b) Mediterranean/Latin-American close-angle conversational seating which the angular threshold may classify as `none` because the chairs are too close to resolve a stable o-space, and (c) Middle-Eastern majlis perimeter seating which is technically circular but at distances that exceed the o-space radius constraint. Downstream model trainers MUST treat L44 outputs as culturally indexed and the secondary Likert as conditionally interpretable on the cultural prior of the depicted scene.
semantics.extraction_notes_3d: In a 3D / VR substrate the extraction is materially easier: chair instances and orientations are already metric, the floor plane is given, and the F-formation o-space is computable as the smallest enclosed region whose boundary contains all chair-front rays within their first 2.5 m. The extractor should take the 3D scene graph, project chair-front normals onto the floor plane, compute pairwise ray intersections (or Voronoi-style closest-approach centroids for parallel-front cases), cluster the intersections by spatial proximity to identify candidate o-space centroids, and emit the largest cluster's centroid as `o_space_centroid_xy` and its enclosing-circle radius as `o_space_radius_m` (note: metric in 3D, pixels in 2D). The arrangement-enum classification applies the same angular thresholds as in 2D but with metric distance gating: vis-à-vis at >3 m intra-chair separation drops the secondary affordance score by 1 step per metre. In VR, occupancy state (whether the avatars are actually present) is observable, but L44 is an *affordance* construct and so should be computed from the seating geometry alone, with avatar occupancy contributing only to a separate confidence-of-realisation field. The `requires_floor_plan: false` setting holds even in 3D: L44 is a local seat-cluster property, not a configurational property of the room.
factor_associations: V2.6 Latent_Indicators sheet, factor cluster F-CONV-1 (Conversation Affordance): rating items "seats face each other", "chairs are clustered", "the arrangement invites conversation"; behavioral proxy "if people sat here, they would talk to each other".

Loads on the broader V2.6 *interactional-affordance* superfactor alongside L43 (Approach Invitation) and L48 (Dyadic Intimacy), with L44 providing the multi-chair angular-convergence component distinct from L43's single-chair approach cue and L48's distance/intimacy cue.
scope_excludes: Single-seat scenes regardless of orientation; sociopetal topology is constitutively a *multi-seat* property.

Parallel-rank seating arrangements (theatre, lecture-hall, transit-bench rows) where all chair-fronts are coplanar and converge only at infinity; these are L52 (presentation) configurations, not sociopetal.

Sociofugal arrangements (airport terminal back-to-back, library carrel rows facing walls) — these are the *inverse* construct and should receive the `none` enum value with secondary score 0, not a low sociopetal score.
Standing-only F-formations without seats; these are within the F-formation tradition but outside the *seating* scope of L44 (and largely covered by L56 mingling affordance).
Workstation/cubicle seating where chairs face screens or work surfaces independently; the convergence is on individual tasks, not on a shared o-space.
scope_includes: Multi-chair arrangements where chair-front normals converge on a shared point or compact region within roughly 3 metres of all participating seats (the F-formation o-space radius constraint).

Arrangements where the o-space is enclosed by the *combination* of seats and a shared focal artefact (table, hearth, screen) rather than by the seats alone, provided at least two seats face inward toward that artefact at angles less than 180° apart.

Side-by-side parallel seating with a small intervening table or shared focal object that re-establishes a partial o-space; classified as the `side_by_side` enum value with a moderate (not maximal) secondary affordance score, per Hall's (1966, ch. 10) observation that this arrangement is the dominant Japanese sociopetal form.
